chunks.title: Big-O and time complexity
chunks.text: Big-O notation describes how an algorithm's runtime grows with input size n in the worst case. Common classes from fastest to slowest: O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n log n) linearithmic, O(n²) quadratic, O(2^n) exponential, O(n!) factorial. Big-Ω is the lower bound, Big-Θ is a tight bound. We drop constants and lower-order terms because they don't change the growth rate as n gets large.

chunks.title: Sorting algorithms
chunks.text: Comparison-based sorts have a lower bound of O(n log n). Merge sort: divide-and-conquer, O(n log n) guaranteed, stable, O(n) extra space. Quicksort: pivot partition, O(n log n) average and O(n²) worst case, in-place, not stable, but usually the fastest in practice. Heap sort: O(n log n) guaranteed, in-place, not stable. Counting sort and radix sort beat O(n log n) by exploiting structure in the data (integer keys with small range).

chunks.title: Binary search
chunks.text: Binary search finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search interval. Compare the middle element to the target; recurse into the left or right half. Time complexity is O(log n) and space is O(1) iteratively or O(log n) recursively. It generalizes to 'search the smallest x such that predicate(x) is true' over any monotonic predicate — useful for problems like 'find minimum capacity to ship within D days'.

chunks.title: Recursion
chunks.text: A recursive function calls itself on a smaller subproblem. Every recursion needs a base case (the smallest subproblem solved directly) and a recursive case (problem reduces to one or more smaller instances). The runtime stack holds one frame per active call, so deep recursion can overflow. Tail recursion can be optimized to a loop by some compilers. Recursion is natural for divide-and-conquer, tree/graph traversal, and backtracking.

chunks.title: Dynamic programming
chunks.text: Dynamic programming solves a problem by combining solutions to overlapping subproblems. Two requirements: optimal substructure (the optimal solution uses optimal solutions to subproblems) and overlapping subproblems (the same subproblem is solved multiple times). Two styles: top-down with memoization (recursion + cache) and bottom-up with tabulation (fill a table iteratively). Classic problems: longest common subsequence, knapsack, edit distance, matrix chain multiplication, Fibonacci.

chunks.title: Greedy algorithms
chunks.text: A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping for a global optimum. They're simple and fast but only correct when the problem has the greedy-choice property and optimal substructure. Examples that work: activity selection, fractional knapsack, Huffman coding, Dijkstra's algorithm, minimum spanning tree (Kruskal, Prim). Examples that don't: 0/1 knapsack — needs DP because the locally best item isn't necessarily in the best overall set.

chunks.title: Graph traversal: DFS and BFS
chunks.text: Depth-first search (DFS) explores as deep as possible before backtracking, using a stack (often the recursion stack). Breadth-first search (BFS) explores by levels using a queue, which makes it the algorithm of choice for shortest path in unweighted graphs. Both are O(V+E). DFS is the basis for topological sort, cycle detection, and finding strongly connected components. BFS is the basis for shortest-path-by-edges, bipartite-check, and level-order traversal.

chunks.title: Dijkstra's shortest path
chunks.text: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths from a source vertex to all others in a graph with non-negative edge weights. It uses a min-heap (priority queue) keyed by current shortest distance. Time complexity is O((V+E) log V) with a binary heap. It fails on graphs with negative edges — use Bellman-Ford (O(VE)) there, which also detects negative cycles. Dijkstra is the routing core in GPS navigation and network packet routing.

chunks.title: Divide and conquer
chunks.text: Divide and conquer splits a problem into independent subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines their solutions. Merge sort and quicksort are canonical. The Master Theorem gives the runtime for recurrences of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n): depending on whether f(n) dominates, matches, or is dominated by n^log_b(a), the answer is f(n), n^log_b(a)·log n, or n^log_b(a) respectively.

chunks.title: Quickselect
chunks.text: Quickselect finds the k-th smallest element in an unsorted array in O(n) average time, faster than sorting (O(n log n)). It uses quicksort's partition step but only recurses into the side containing position k. Worst case is O(n²) with bad pivots; the median-of-medians pivot choice guarantees O(n) worst case but is rarely used in practice because the constant is high.

chunks.title: Two pointers technique
chunks.text: The two-pointer technique uses two indices that move through a sequence under different rules to solve problems in O(n). Classic uses: detecting pairs that sum to a target in a sorted array (move pointers inward), reversing a string in place, removing duplicates from sorted input, and the partition step in quicksort. Two pointers replace nested loops that would otherwise run in O(n²).

chunks.title: Sliding window
chunks.text: Sliding window maintains a contiguous subarray defined by left and right pointers, advancing the right pointer to grow the window and the left to shrink it. It solves substring problems (longest substring without repeating characters), fixed-size window problems (max average of k consecutive elements), and counting problems in O(n). The trick is choosing the right invariant to maintain while sliding.

chunks.title: Backtracking
chunks.text: Backtracking systematically explores a state space by extending partial solutions and undoing the extension if a constraint is violated. Classic problems: N-queens, sudoku, generating permutations and subsets, the knight's tour. Pruning (skipping branches that can't lead to a solution) is the key to making backtracking practical. The worst case is exponential, but pruning often cuts the explored space dramatically.

chunks.title: Bit manipulation
chunks.text: Bit manipulation exploits binary representation for compact algorithms: AND (&) clears bits, OR (|) sets, XOR (^) toggles, NOT (~) inverts, shifts (<<, >>) move. Idioms: x & (x-1) clears the lowest set bit, x & -x isolates it, x ^ y swaps without a temp. Bitmasks represent subsets of a small set compactly, enabling DP over subsets in O(2^n · n).

chunks.title: Prefix sums
chunks.text: A prefix sum array stores the cumulative sum up to index i: prefix[i] = arr[0] + ... + arr[i]. Range sum queries on a static array become O(1): sum(l, r) = prefix[r] − prefix[l−1]. Extends to 2D (image processing, sub-matrix sums). The idea generalises to prefix XOR, prefix product, and prefix maximum. Pair with Fenwick trees when the array is mutable.

chunks.title: Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest path
chunks.text: Floyd-Warshall computes shortest paths between every pair of vertices in a graph in O(V³). It runs three nested loops over an intermediate vertex k and updates dist[i][j] = min(dist[i][j], dist[i][k] + dist[k][j]). Works with negative edges (no negative cycles). Often slower than running Dijkstra V times, but the code is simple and it handles negatives, making it useful for small dense graphs.

chunks.title: Bellman-Ford
chunks.text: Bellman-Ford finds shortest paths from a single source in O(VE), allowing negative edge weights. It relaxes every edge V−1 times. A V-th pass that still relaxes an edge proves the graph has a negative cycle. Used in distance-vector routing protocols (RIP) and as a sanity check before forex/arbitrage detection.

chunks.title: Topological sort
chunks.text: Topological sort linearises a DAG so every edge u→v has u before v. Two algorithms: Kahn's algorithm (repeatedly pick a node with in-degree 0, decrement neighbours) and DFS-based (push nodes onto a stack on post-order, then pop). Both run in O(V+E). Cycle detection comes free — if Kahn's leaves nodes with in-degree > 0, or DFS sees a back edge, the graph isn't a DAG. Used in build systems, course schedulers, and task graphs.

chunks.title: Strongly connected components
chunks.text: An SCC is a maximal subset of vertices where every node is reachable from every other. Tarjan's algorithm finds all SCCs in O(V+E) using a single DFS, low-link values, and an explicit stack. Kosaraju's algorithm runs DFS on the original graph, then on the transpose. Both produce the same SCCs. The condensation of a graph (each SCC contracted to a node) is always a DAG.

chunks.title: KMP string matching
chunks.text: Knuth-Morris-Pratt finds occurrences of a pattern P in a text T in O(|P| + |T|). The trick is precomputing the failure function (longest proper prefix of P that's also a suffix), which tells the algorithm how far to shift the pattern after a mismatch without re-scanning text characters. The failure function takes O(|P|) to build. KMP avoids the O(|P|·|T|) of naive matching.

chunks.title: Rabin-Karp rolling hash
chunks.text: Rabin-Karp finds a pattern in text using a rolling hash that updates in O(1) per slide. Hash matches are double-checked against the actual pattern to handle collisions. Average and best cases are O(|P| + |T|); worst case (constant collisions) is O(|P|·|T|). The real win is finding multiple patterns at once, since you can probe a set of hashes per window.

chunks.title: Z-algorithm
chunks.text: The Z-algorithm computes, for each position i in a string, the length of the longest substring starting at i that matches a prefix of the string. Built in O(n) using a sliding window of the rightmost-known matching prefix. Z-array + pattern + '$' + text gives O(|P| + |T|) substring matching like KMP, often easier to derive than the failure function.

chunks.title: Knapsack 0/1
chunks.text: The 0/1 knapsack problem asks for the max value subset of items, each with a weight and value, that fits in capacity W. Solved by DP in O(nW): dp[i][w] = max(dp[i−1][w], dp[i−1][w−wi] + vi). Space can be reduced to O(W) by iterating w in reverse. NP-hard in general (pseudo-polynomial); fractional knapsack — where you can take fractions of items — is greedy and runs in O(n log n).

chunks.title: Edit distance (Levenshtein)
chunks.text: Edit distance is the minimum number of single-character edits (insert, delete, substitute) to transform one string into another. DP recurrence: dp[i][j] depends on dp[i−1][j], dp[i][j−1], dp[i−1][j−1]. Runs in O(|s| · |t|) time and space. Used in spell-check, DNA alignment, autocomplete ranking. Hirschberg's algorithm reduces space to O(min(|s|, |t|)) at no extra time cost.

chunks.title: Reservoir sampling
chunks.text: Reservoir sampling selects k elements uniformly at random from a stream of unknown length. Algorithm R: keep the first k items, then for each item i > k, replace a random reservoir slot with probability k/i. Each item ends up with probability k/n of being in the reservoir. Used in large-data sampling, log analysis, and stream analytics where storing the full dataset is infeasible.

chunks.title: A* search
chunks.text: A* finds shortest paths in a weighted graph using a priority queue keyed by f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g is the actual cost so far and h is an admissible heuristic estimating the remaining cost. With a consistent heuristic, A* is optimal and expands fewer nodes than Dijkstra. Heuristic choice matters: Manhattan distance for grid paths, Euclidean for free 2D space, custom for domain problems.

chunks.title: Randomized algorithms
chunks.text: Randomized algorithms use random choices to achieve fast expected runtime, correct expected output, or simpler design. Examples: randomized quicksort (random pivot avoids worst case on sorted input), Karger's minimum cut, Miller-Rabin primality test. Las Vegas algorithms are always correct, random in runtime; Monte Carlo are always fast, random in output. They're often simpler than the deterministic counterparts.

chunks.title: Approximation algorithms
chunks.text: For NP-hard problems where exact answers are too slow, approximation algorithms return solutions provably within a factor of optimal in polynomial time. Example: a 2-approximation for vertex cover by picking both endpoints of any uncovered edge. PTAS (polynomial-time approximation scheme) gets within (1+ε) of optimal for any ε. Used in routing, scheduling, and clustering when optimal is infeasible.

chunks.title: NP-completeness
chunks.text: NP-complete problems are the hardest in NP — if any one has a polynomial algorithm, all do (P = NP). Classic examples: SAT, 3-SAT, vertex cover, Hamiltonian cycle, knapsack, TSP, graph colouring. Proving NP-completeness is by reduction: show that an existing NP-complete problem reduces in polynomial time to yours. Recognising NP-completeness saves time — stop hunting for an efficient exact algorithm and switch to approximation, heuristics, or special-case methods.

chunks.title: Tarjan's algorithm for articulation points
chunks.text: Tarjan's DFS finds articulation points (cut vertices) and bridges (cut edges) in O(V+E). It tracks disc[v] (DFS discovery time) and low[v] (smallest disc reachable via at most one back edge from v's subtree). A non-root v is an articulation point if it has a child u with low[u] >= disc[v]. Used in network reliability — which routers, if down, would partition the network?

chunks.title: Counting sort and radix sort
chunks.text: Counting sort handles integer keys in a small range [0, k] in O(n + k) time by counting occurrences and reconstructing the sorted output. Radix sort sorts integers by processing digits from least to most significant using a stable sort (usually counting sort) at each digit. Radix sort runs in O(d · (n + b)) for d digits in base b — effectively linear when d is small.

chunks.title: Quicksort partitioning schemes
chunks.text: Lomuto partition is simpler: pick a pivot, scan once swapping elements smaller than the pivot to the front. Hoare partition uses two pointers from both ends moving inward and is usually faster (fewer swaps). Three-way partitioning (Dutch national flag) groups elements into <, =, > the pivot — important when arrays have many duplicates, where standard quicksort degrades to O(n²).

chunks.title: Mathematical induction in algorithm proofs
chunks.text: Inductive proofs justify recursive algorithms: prove correctness on the base case, then assume correctness on smaller inputs and prove correctness on the current input. Strong induction (assume for all smaller) is often needed for divide-and-conquer. Loop invariants serve the same role for iterative algorithms — a property true before, during, and after each iteration.

chunks.title: Algorithm design paradigms
chunks.text: Most algorithms fall into one of a few paradigms: brute force (try everything), divide and conquer (split + combine), dynamic programming (overlapping subproblems), greedy (local optimum), backtracking (search + prune), branch and bound (DP variant for optimization), reduction (map your problem to a known one). When tackling a new problem, walk through the paradigms — usually one of them fits.
